summary: Luu revisits Butler Lampson's 1999 assessment of computer science research to evaluate what's changed 16 years later. He methodically analyzes each category (Yes/Maybe/No) from Lampson's original talk, finding that nearly all the 1999 'Yes' items remain fundamental today, while seven formerly uncertain or failed technologies (like garbage collection, distributed systems, and RPC) became mainstream successes. Only RISC moved from 'Maybe' to 'No' as x86 dominated despite predictions of its demise. The analysis demonstrates that the landscape has changed substantially within a single career, making long-term predictions about research impact fundamentally unreliable.
key_points: All technologies rated 'Yes' in 1999 remain fundamental today (virtual memory, packet nets, RDB/SQL, transactions, GUIs, web, algorithms)
Seven technologies upgraded from 'Maybe' or 'No' to mainstream success: garbage collection, reuse, capabilities, RPC, and distributed computing most notably
RISC processors failed to dominate despite 1980s-90s predictions, with x86 winning through engineering rather than ISA elegance
Parallelism remains frustratingly difficult - Lampson admitted threading/locks were a failed solution and parallel programming still requires 'wizards'
Fancy type systems and pure functional programming remain niche despite their appeal to enthusiasts
money_quotes: Lampson's comment was that when they came up with threading, locks, and conditional variables at PARC, they thought they were creating something that programmers could use to take advantage of parallelism, but that they now have decades of evidence that they were wrong.

A clean RISC ISA is a beautiful thing. I sometimes re-read Dick Sites's explanation of the Alpha design just to admire it, but it turns out beauty isn't really critical for the commercial success of an ISA.
I take Lampson's view, which is that if the vast majority of programmers are literally incapable of using a certain class of technologies, that class of technologies has probably not succeeded.

After reviewing Lampson's 1999 talk, I'm much more optimistic about research areas that haven't yielded much real-world impact (yet), like capability based computing and fancy type systems. It seems basically impossible to predict what areas will become valuable over the next thirty years.
Distributed systems are so ubiquitous that startups with zero distributed systems expertise regularly use distributed systems provided by Amazon or Microsoft, and it's totally fine.
key_insight: The rapid transformation from research concepts to mainstream adoption within a single career span makes long-term technological predictions essentially impossible, suggesting humility about dismissing today's academic research areas as impractical.
